text: Multiple Encryption & DES

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: a replacement for DES was needed

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: – theoretical attacks that can break it – demonstrated exhaustive key search attacks

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: AES is a new cipher alternative • prior to this alternative was to use multiple encryption with DES implementations • Triple-DES is the chosen form

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Double-DES?

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ The simplest form of multiple encryption has two encryption stages and two keys e.g. double-DES

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: C = EK2(EK1(P))

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ However, it is subject to the meet in the middle (MitM) attack

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: “Meet-in-the-Middle” Attack (MitM)

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢The MitM attack on double encryption for any symmetric cipher such as DES and AES is possible with almost the same computing effort as breaking a single encryption.

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢This effort is in the order of 2N, where N is the number of bits in the encryption key.

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: “Meet-in-the-Middle” Attack

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Double DES is subject to the “meet-in-the-middle” (MitM) attack which works whenever a cipher is used twice

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ The attacker obtains a pair (P, C) where C is the

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ciphertext of the plaintext P.

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ in double DES we have: X = EK1(P) = DK2(C) ➢ attack by encrypting P with all keys and store then

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: decrypt C with keys and match X value

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ MitM takes O(256) steps to break the 2 keys ➢ a brute force attack takes O(2112) steps to break the 2

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: keys of DES

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Detailed steps to do a MitM attack:

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf
SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Find a pair of plaintext and ciphertext “P,C”.

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf
SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: For all possible N values of the 1st unknown key K1 prepare a list {Xe ; e= 1,2,3,…., N}, where Xe = E(ke, P).

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: 3. Generate Xd = D(kd, C) for possible values of the 2nd unknown key k2 , where {d= 1,2,3,….} Compare each generated Xd with the prepared list until you find a matching value such that E(ke, P) = D(kd, C). The unknown keys are: K1 = Ke and K2 = Kd. 5.

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf
SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: 6. Use a second pair of plaintext and ciphertext to verify the obtained keys

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Triple-DES with Two-Keys (standardized in ANSI X9.17 & ISO8732)

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: • would seem to need 3 distinct keys but can use 2 keys with E-D-E sequence.

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: to avoid MitM attack we must use 3 encryptions

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: C = EK1(DK2(EK1(P)))

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf
SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: the use of encryption & decryption stages is equivalent in security, but the chosen E-D-E structure allows for compatibility with single-DES implementations. if K1=K2 then can work with single DES

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: • no current known practical attacks • suffers from being 3 times slower to run

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Triple-DES with Two-Keys (The E – D – E Structure)

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: To break this arrangement using MitM, the attacker needs a computing effort in the order of 2 N+N, where N is the number of bits in the encryption key.

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Triple-DES with Three-Keys

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: although are no practical attacks on two-key, Triple- DES have some concerns

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: a preferred alternative is to use Triple-DES with Three- Keys to avoid any concerns

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: C = EK3(DK2(EK1(P))) • has been adopted by some Internet applications, e.g.

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: PGP, S/MIME

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Modes of Operation

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: block ciphers encrypt fixed size blocks

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: – e.g. DES encrypts 64-bit blocks with 56-bit key • need some way to encrypt/decrypt arbitrary amounts

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: of data in practice

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: NIST SP 800-38A defines 5 modes • have block and stream modes to cover a wide variety of applications

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: can be used with any block cipher

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Electronic Codebook Book (ECB)

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: message is broken into independent blocks which are encrypted

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: each block is a value which is substituted, like a codebook, hence name

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: each block is encoded independently of the other blocks Ci = EK(Pi) this mode is used for secure transmission of single values

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf
SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Electronic Codebook Mode (ECB)

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Advantages and Limitations of ECB

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ message repetitions may show in ciphertext

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: if aligned with message block • particularly with data such as graphics • or with messages that change very little, which become a codebook analysis problem

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ weakness is due to the encrypted message blocks

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: being independent

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ main use is sending a few blocks of data, e.g. a session

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: encryption key

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ECB Limitations

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Alice Hates ECB Mode Alice’s uncompressed image, and ECB encrypted

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ❑Why does this happen?

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ❑Same plaintext yields same ciphertext!

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Cipher Block Chaining (CBC)

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: message is broken into blocks • • each previous cipher blocks is chained with current plaintext block, hence name

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: use Initial Vector (IV) to start process; i=1,2,3,….

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Ci = EK(Pi  Ci-1) C0 = IV

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Uses: bulk data encryption, authentication

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Cipher Block Chaining (CBC)

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Ci = EK(Pi  Ci-1)

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: C0 = IV

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Alice Likes CBC Mode Alice’s uncompressed image, Alice CBC encrypted

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ❑Why does this happen?

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ❑Same plaintext yields different ciphertext!

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Message Padding

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: At end of message we must handle a possible last short block.

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ which is not as large as block size of cipher ➢ pad either with known non-data value (e.g. nulls) ➢ or pad last block along with count of pad size

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Consider the last block containing only 3 bytes of data: [D1] , [D2] , and [D3].

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: To complete the block to be 8 bytes (64 bits) we must add 5 more bytes.

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: The padded block will be: {[D1] [D2] [D3] [0] [0] [0] [0] [5]} • This means we have 3 data bytes, then 4 bytes EACH CONTAINS ZERO + last byte contains the count of added padding bytes (which is 5).

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ this method may require an extra entire block over those in

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: message. (Why??)

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Advantages and Limitations of CBC

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ a ciphertext block depends on all blocks before it ➢ any change to a block affects all following ciphertext blocks ➢ need Initialization Vector (IV)

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: which must be known to sender & receiver •

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: if sent in clear, attacker can change bits of first block, and change IV to compensate

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: hence IV must either be a fixed value (as in EFTPOS) • or must be sent encrypted in ECB mode before rest of message

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Stream Modes of Operation

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ Block modes encrypt entire block ➢ Stream modes convert block cipher into stream cipher

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf
SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf
SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: cipher feedback (CFB) mode • allows to operate on smaller plaintext units, e.g. real time data • if a transmission error occurs in one ciphertext block, there will be an error in several blocks of the decrypted plaintext (this is called error propagation) • it uses a complex structure output feedback (OFB) mode • is simpler than CFB but preferred to operate on entire data

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: blocks

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf
SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: counter (CTR) mode • is like OFB but provides more advantages

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ Encryption is done by XORing plaintext blocks with random bits ➢ Use block cipher as some form of pseudo-random number generator to

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: generate the required random bits

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Output FeedBack (OFB) Mode

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: message is treated as a stream of bits • output of cipher is added to message • output is then feed back (hence name) • feedback is independent of message • can be computed in advance Oi = EK(Oi-1) Ci = Pi  Oi O0 = IV

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Uses: stream encryption on noisy channels

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Output FeedBack (OFB)

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Oi = EK(Oi-1) Ci = Pi  Oi O0 = IV

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: (IV is the nonce which is a random number used only once)

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Advantages and Limitations of OFB

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ needs an IV (nonce) which is unique for each use ➢ if ever reuse IV, attacker can recover outputs ➢ bit errors do not propagate ➢ more vulnerable to message stream modification attack than

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: is CFB

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ sender & receiver must remain in synchronism, or all data is

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: lost

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ only use with full block feedback, where typically a block is 64

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: or 128 bits

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Counter (CTR) Mode

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf
SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: the Counter (CTR) mode is a variant of OFB, but which encrypts a counter value (hence name) rather than any feedback value

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: must have a different key & counter value for every plaintext block (never reused) Oi = EK(Counteri ) Ci = Pi  Oi Uses: high-speed network encryptions

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Counter (CTR)

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Oi = EK(Counteri ) Ci = Pi  Oi

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf
SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf
SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Counter1 is a random number Counteri+1 = Counteri + 1

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: Advantages and Limitations of CTR

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: ➢ efficiency

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf

text: can do parallel encryptions in h/w or s/w • can preprocess in advance of need • good for bursty high speed links ➢ random access to encrypted data blocks ➢ provable security (good as other modes) ➢ but must ensure never reuse key/counter values, otherwise could break (like OFB)

metadata.filename: SEC 422 CH 6 Block Cipher Operation - audio.pdf
